title: AI Product Strategy
description: Learn to evaluate AI opportunities, define success metrics, and build compelling product strategies — from identifying the right use cases to communicating AI capabilities to stakeholders.
learningObjectives: Build and prioritize an AI feature roadmap grounded in real user value and engineering feasibility
Define meaningful success metrics that capture AI feature performance beyond traditional product KPIs
Communicate AI capabilities, limitations, and tradeoffs effectively to engineering, leadership, and customers

sections.title: Building an AI Feature Roadmap
sections.content:
Building an AI feature roadmap is fundamentally different from a traditional product roadmap. AI features carry more uncertainty, require iteration cycles that don't fit neatly into sprints, and often improve gradually rather than shipping as "done."

The most important principle is to **start with the problem, not the technology.** The most common mistake PMs make is starting with "we should add AI" instead of "our users struggle with X." Before any AI feature hits your roadmap, you should be able to answer: What user pain point does this solve, and why is Claude the right tool for it?

**The Build vs. Buy Decision**

For every AI capability on your roadmap, you face a three-way decision. You can **use Claude's API directly**, which gives you the most control over custom workflows, unique data handling, and tight product integration — but you also own all the complexity. Alternatively, you can **use a pre-built solution** — third-party tools built on top of LLMs that are faster to ship but offer less differentiation and control. Finally, and this option is easy to overlook, you can **choose not to build it at all.** Sometimes the non-AI solution is simply better. A well-designed filter can outperform a natural language search if your users already know what they're looking for.

**Prioritization Framework for AI Features**

Once you've identified candidate AI features, rank each one across four dimensions:

• **User value** — How painful is the problem today? How frequently do users hit it?
• **Feasibility** — Can Claude handle this reliably today? Do you have the data and infrastructure?
• **Risk tolerance** — What happens when the AI is wrong? A wrong product recommendation is annoying; a wrong medical summary is dangerous.
• **Iteration speed** — How quickly can you test and improve the feature? Prefer features where you can measure quality and iterate on prompts without redeploying.

Beyond scoring individual features, **sequencing matters.** Ship AI features that build user trust first — lower-stakes, high-visibility wins like summarization or smart categorization. These early wins establish confidence. Once users trust the AI in your product, you can introduce higher-stakes features like automated actions or decision support, and users will be more willing to rely on them.

sections.title: Defining Success Metrics for AI Features
sections.content:
Traditional product metrics don't fully capture whether an AI feature is working. You need a layered approach that measures accuracy, user impact, and business value — because an AI feature can be technically accurate but still useless to users. Think of these layers as a pyramid: output quality forms the foundation, user experience sits in the middle, and business impact crowns the top.

**Layer 1: Output Quality Metrics**

The foundation of any AI feature is whether Claude is producing good outputs. **Accuracy** measures what percentage of outputs are factually correct or match expected results — and this requires a human evaluation process, at least initially, because automated checks alone can miss subtle errors. You also need to track **hallucination rate** explicitly: how often does Claude generate information that isn't grounded in the provided context? This is distinct from general accuracy because a response can sound perfectly reasonable while being entirely fabricated. Finally, monitor **consistency** — whether the same input produces reliably similar outputs. Some variance is fine for creative tasks, but structured outputs like data extraction or classification should be stable across repeated runs.

**Layer 2: User Experience Metrics**

Output quality tells you if Claude is technically working, but user experience metrics tell you if it's actually useful. The single most important metric for most AI features is **acceptance rate**: when Claude suggests something, how often do users accept it versus edit or dismiss it? This one number captures whether the AI is genuinely helping. To go deeper, measure **edit distance** — when users do modify Claude's output, how much do they change? Small edits mean Claude is getting close to what users need, while complete rewrites signal that it's missing the mark entirely. Round out this layer with **time saved**: does the AI feature actually reduce the time to complete the task? Be sure to measure the full workflow, not just the generation step, since a fast AI response that requires heavy editing may not save time overall.

**Layer 3: Business Impact Metrics**

The top of the pyramid connects AI performance to business outcomes. Track **task completion rate** to see if users are finishing their overall jobs more successfully with the AI feature. Monitor **retention and engagement** to determine whether users who interact with AI features come back more often. And calculate **cost per outcome** — what each Claude API call costs relative to the value it delivers — because a feature that's beloved by users but costs $5 per interaction may not be sustainable.

**Good vs. Bad AI Metrics**

The difference between good and bad AI metrics comes down to whether you're measuring activity or impact. Saying "Claude responded in under 2 seconds" tracks latency, but speed without quality is worthless. A better metric is "78% of Claude-generated summaries were accepted without edits," which directly measures value delivered. Similarly, "we processed 10,000 requests through Claude" measures volume, not impact — whereas "support agents resolved tickets 40% faster when using Claude-suggested responses" ties the AI directly to a business outcome.

sections.title: Communicating AI to Stakeholders
sections.content:
As a PM, you sit at the intersection of engineering, leadership, and customers — each of whom has a different relationship with AI. Your job is to translate between these groups without overpromising or underselling. Getting this communication right is often what separates AI features that get adopted from those that get abandoned.

**Talking to Leadership**

Executives often oscillate between "AI will change everything" and "why isn't it perfect yet?" Your job is to anchor them in reality. Start by **framing AI as a capability, not a feature.** Instead of "we're adding AI," say "we're reducing the time it takes customers to draft reports by 60%." Leaders care about outcomes, not technology labels. You also need to **set expectations about iteration** — AI features improve over time through prompt refinement, user feedback, and model updates. The v1 won't be the final version, and that's by design, not a failure. Finally, **be upfront about costs.** Claude API calls cost money, and model inference at scale is a real line item. Present the cost-per-value equation, not just the cost in isolation, so leadership can evaluate ROI with full context.

**Talking to Customers**

Customer communication requires a different kind of precision. Never say "AI-powered" and leave it at that — tell users what the feature actually does. For example: "This summary was generated automatically from your meeting transcript. You can edit it before sharing." That single sentence communicates the capability, the source, and the user's control all at once. **Transparency builds trust**, so let users see when Claude generated something and give them easy ways to edit, regenerate, or dismiss AI output. Trust comes from control, not from hiding the machinery. You'll also need to **manage the "magic" expectations** that users bring from flashy AI demos. Set the right frame early: "This feature handles about 80% of cases automatically. For the rest, it gives you a starting point." That honest framing prevents disappointment and actually increases satisfaction with the feature.

**The Limitation Communication Framework**

When you need to explain what Claude can't do — and you will — use a three-part structure that keeps the conversation constructive. **Lead with what it does well**, being positive and specific: "Claude is excellent at analyzing your customer feedback and identifying themes." Then **acknowledge where it has boundaries**, being honest without being defeatist: "It works best with English text and may miss nuance in highly technical jargon." Finally, **show how you're addressing it** to demonstrate a path forward: "We're adding domain-specific examples to improve accuracy in your industry's terminology." This structure lets you be transparent about limitations while maintaining confidence in your product direction.

sections.title: Scoping AI Features with Engineering
sections.content:
Scoping AI features requires a different conversation with engineering than traditional features. The uncertainty is higher, the iteration cycles are different, and the PM needs to understand enough about Claude's capabilities to make informed tradeoff decisions. You don't need to become an engineer, but you do need enough technical literacy to be a credible partner in these conversations.

**Prompt Engineering Decisions PMs Should Care About**

You don't need to write the prompts yourself, but you need to understand the tradeoffs that prompt design creates. The **system prompt** defines Claude's personality, constraints, and behavior in your product. As a PM, you should review and approve system prompts because they directly shape the user experience — a system prompt is essentially your product's voice when it speaks through AI. **Output format** is another decision with product implications: should Claude return structured JSON or natural language? Structured output is more reliable for programmatic use, while natural language is better for user-facing content. The choice affects both the user experience and engineering complexity. Finally, **guardrails and boundaries** — what Claude should refuse to do in your product, which topics it should avoid — are fundamentally product decisions, not engineering decisions. You own these choices because they define the user experience and your product's brand.

**Understanding Cost and Latency Tradeoffs**

One of the most important technical conversations you'll have with engineering is about the relationship between model selection, cost, and performance. **Model selection** directly shapes this tradeoff: Claude Haiku is fast and cheap, making it great for real-time autocomplete or classification tasks. Claude Sonnet balances capability and speed, fitting most product features well. Claude Opus is the most capable but slower and more expensive — reserve it for complex reasoning tasks where quality justifies the cost.

Beyond model choice, **input length affects cost** significantly. Every token in the prompt costs money, so if you're sending a 50-page document to Claude on every request, that adds up fast. Work with engineering to optimize what context actually needs to be sent. On the UX side, consider **streaming vs. complete responses**: streaming shows users output as it's generated, making Claude feel faster even though the total generation time is the same. For user-facing features, streaming is almost always the right choice because perceived speed matters as much as actual speed.

**Scoping the Iteration Plan**

Unlike traditional features where you scope once and build, AI features ship with a built-in iteration plan that evolves through three phases:

• **V1: Prompt-only approach.** Start with well-crafted prompts and Claude's base capabilities. This ships fast and gives you real user data.
• **V2: Refined with real data.** Use actual user interactions to identify failure modes and improve prompts.
• **V3: Infrastructure investment.** Add retrieval (RAG), fine-tuning, or caching only after you've validated the feature with real users.

As a PM, resist the temptation to over-engineer v1. The fastest path to learning is getting real users interacting with Claude and measuring what works. Every week spent building infrastructure before you've validated the use case is a week you could have spent learning from actual user behavior.

sections.title: Draft an AI Feature PRD
sections.content: Now put your AI product strategy skills to work by drafting a real PRD section for a Claude-powered feature.

exercise.prompt:
Pick a product you use regularly (or one you're building). Draft a PRD section for one AI-powered feature that uses Claude. Your PRD section should include:

1. **Problem Statement** — What user pain point does this feature address? Why is Claude the right solution?
2. **Feature Description** — What does the feature do? How does the user interact with it?
3. **Success Metrics** — Define 3 metrics across different layers (output quality, user experience, and business impact).
4. **Scope and Tradeoffs** — Which Claude model would you use and why? What are the key tradeoffs you're making in v1?
5. **Stakeholder Summary** — Write a 2-sentence description of this feature for your VP of Product who doesn't have a technical background.

Be specific and practical — avoid generic statements like "it will improve efficiency."
hints: Choose a feature where Claude's strengths (language understanding, analysis, generation) match a real user need
For success metrics, think about acceptance rate, edit distance, and task completion — not just accuracy
When choosing a model, consider the tradeoff between quality, latency, and cost for your specific use case
Your stakeholder summary should focus on the user outcome, not the technology
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A specific, well-defined problem statement that connects a real user pain point to Claude's capabilities — not a generic 'AI would be cool here' pitch. (2) A clear feature description with enough detail to understand the user interaction model. (3) Three layered success metrics — at least one measuring output quality (e.g., accuracy, hallucination rate), one measuring user experience (e.g., acceptance rate, time saved), and one measuring business impact (e.g., retention, cost per outcome). (4) A justified model selection (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) with reasoning about the cost-latency-quality tradeoff and at least one explicit v1 scope tradeoff. (5) A stakeholder summary that communicates value without jargon. Deduct points for vague metrics like 'improved efficiency' without specifics, or for choosing a model without justification.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
AI product strategy for PMs comes down to four core principles.

First, **start with the problem, not the technology.** Every AI feature on your roadmap should trace back to a specific user pain point where Claude's capabilities are a genuine fit. If you can't articulate the problem clearly without mentioning AI, the feature probably doesn't belong on your roadmap yet.

Second, **measure what matters across all three layers.** Output quality metrics tell you if Claude is working correctly. User experience metrics tell you if it's actually useful. Business impact metrics tell you if it's worth the investment. You need all three layers because a feature can succeed at one level while failing at another.

Third, **communicate with precision to every audience.** Set realistic expectations with every stakeholder group. Frame AI as a capability that improves over time, not a magic switch you flip on. The way you talk about your AI features shapes whether people trust them.

Fourth, **scope for learning, not perfection.** Ship a prompt-based v1 fast, measure real user behavior, and iterate. The biggest risk in AI product development isn't shipping something imperfect — it's spending six months building infrastructure for a feature users don't want.
